attributes.stem:
A one-week-old girl is being discharged from hospital after recent diagnosis of neonatal lupus erythematosus. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy, and echocardiogram was normal. Her immunisations are up to date. There is no hepatic or cardiac involvement.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are erythematous, annular, slightly scaly plaques with central clearing on her face, particularly in the periorbital distribution, and scattered lesions on her scalp.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate ongoing management?
attributes.explanation:
This baby has been diagnosed with neonatal lupus erythematosus. This is a passively-acquired autoimmune disease from maternal immunoglobulin G with cutaneous symptoms and can have cardiac involvement. It presents with a rash from birth or within the first few weeks of life of annular, erythematous plaques over the head and neck typically, petechiae, telangiectasis and periocular oedema causing a 'raccoon-eye'. The rash is photosensitive. When infants present solely with cutaneous NLE, the prognosis is good with resolution without scarring within 6 - 12 months as the maternal autoantibodies disappear. Thus supportive management with the rash is most appropriate for patient with only cutaneous NLE.

Sun protection and topical steroids represent the most appropriate first-line management for cutaneous NLE. Sun protection is crucial as UV exposure can trigger or worsen the rash. Mild topical steroids can help manage inflammation and symptoms if required.

Systemic corticosteroids would be excessive for isolated cutaneous NLE without systemic involvement. These medications carry significant risks in infants and are reserved for severe cases or those with internal organ involvement, particularly cardiac manifestations.

Hydroxychloroquine may be used prophylactically during pregnancy in mothers with known autoantibodies to prevent cardiac NLE in the fetus, but it is not typically indicated for treating isolated cutaneous NLE after birth.

Oral antibiotics would not be appropriate as this is not an infectious condition. The annular appearance might suggest tinea, but the maternal history and photosensitivity point clearly to NLE.

Phototherapy would be contraindicated as UV exposure worsens the condition. The goal of management is to protect the infant from sun exposure until the condition resolves spontaneously.

Regular cardiac monitoring is essential in these infants, even if initial cardiac examination is normal, as congenital heart block can develop and can be fatal.
learningPoints: Cutaneous neonatal lupus erythematosus typically resolves spontaneously within 6-8 months without scarring.
Sun protection and topical steroids are the mainstay of treatment for cutaneous neonatal lupus.
references: Mitchell G. Neonatal lupus erythematosus.  DermNet NZ.  2021.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/neonatal-lupus-erythematosus
Hon KL, Leung AK. Neonatal lupus erythematosus. Autoimmune Dis. 2012;2012:301274. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3437607/

Yokogawa N, Sumitomo N, Miura M, Shibuya K, Nagai H, Goto M, Murashima A. Neonatal lupus erythematosus. Japanese Journal of Clinical Immunology. 2017: 40 (2). Available at: https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/jsci/40/2/40_124/_article/-char/en
